title: Retrieval Pipelines
lesson:
A retrieval pipeline is the assembly line that turns a user's question into a short, tidy stack of relevant text, ready to hand to a language model. RAG (giving a model your own documents at answer-time) is the big idea; the retrieval pipeline is the plumbing that actually fetches the right documents. The model only ever sees what the pipeline hands it. So if the pipeline grabs junk, even the smartest model writes a confident, well-phrased wrong answer. The retrieval pipeline is where most RAG systems quietly succeed or fail.

Think of a busy restaurant kitchen. A customer orders; the expeditor reads the ticket, runs to the right stations, gathers exactly the ingredients the dish needs, throws out anything off, and plates it before it reaches the chef. The chef (the model) is brilliant but lazy: it cooks only with what is on the counter. A retrieval pipeline is that expeditor for text.

The stages of the line

Here is the path a question travels, step by step:

  1. Query processing. Clean and maybe rewrite the question. A chatty follow-up like "what about the second one?" means nothing on its own, so the pipeline rewrites it using earlier chat into something self-contained.
  2. Embed the query. Turn the question into an embedding — a list of numbers that captures its meaning — so it can be compared to stored text by meaning, not exact words.
  3. Search. Look through the stored chunks (the small passages your documents were sliced into) and pull the closest matches. This is often a mix of semantic search (meaning-based, via a vector database) and old-fashioned keyword search.
  4. Rerank. Take the ~50 rough candidates and have a slower, sharper model score each one against the question, keeping only the top handful.
  5. Assemble the context. Stitch the survivors into a clean block, drop duplicates, fit it inside the context window (the model's limited reading space), and pass it to the model.

Why two searches beat one

Semantic search is great at meaning but can miss exact things — a part number like X-4471, a rare surname, a specific error code. Keyword search nails those literal hits but is blind to wording it has never seen. Combining both is called hybrid search. Picture finding a house: GPS gets you to the right neighbourhood (meaning), and the street number on the door confirms the exact place (keywords). You want both, then you blend the two ranked lists into one. A common blending recipe is RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion) — it rewards results that score high in either list, without you needing to tune fiddly weights.

Reranking: the second, pickier pass

The first search is fast but rough — it judges each chunk on its own. A reranker (also called a cross-encoder) is slower but reads the question and one chunk together, so it understands them as a pair and scores relevance far more accurately. The trick is to use the cheap search to fetch many candidates, then the expensive reranker to pick the best few. It is like a recruiter: a keyword filter narrows 10,000 CVs to 50, then a human reads those 50 closely to choose 5. Reranking is one of the highest-leverage upgrades you can add.

def retrieve(query, k=5):
    q_vec = embed(query)                       # 2. embed
    dense = vdb.search(q_vec, top=50)          # 3a. meaning search
    sparse = keyword_search(query, top=50)     # 3b. keyword search
    fused = reciprocal_rank_fusion(dense, sparse)  # blend lists
    ranked = reranker.score(query, fused)      # 4. pickier pass
    return ranked[:k]                          # 5. keep the best few

That k — how many chunks you keep — is a balancing act. Too few and you starve the model of facts; too many and you bury the answer in noise and burn tokens, which costs money and slows everything down. Five to ten focused chunks usually beats twenty sloppy ones. More is not better; cleaner is better.

Where this lives in a real product

A support bot, a "chat with your PDFs" tool, an internal company search, a coding assistant that pulls in your repo — all run a retrieval pipeline behind the scenes. The same machinery powers AI memory systems, where the "documents" are past conversations the assistant retrieves to remember you. In every case the rule holds: garbage in, garbage out. Spend your effort making sure the right text reaches the model, and measure it — does the correct chunk actually land in the top results? If it does, good answers tend to follow.

💡 Build the dumb version first: one embedding search, top 5 chunks, no reranking. Get it working end to end, see where it fails, then add hybrid search and reranking to fix the specific misses you observe.
mentalModel: A retrieval pipeline is the kitchen expeditor: it reads the order, sprints to the right stations, gathers and double-checks exactly the ingredients the dish needs, and plates them before the chef. The model is a brilliant but lazy chef who only cooks with what is on the counter — so the whole meal depends on what the expeditor brought.
mistakes: Skipping reranking. The first fast search judges each chunk alone and is surprisingly noisy. Adding a reranker that reads the question and chunk together is often the single biggest quality jump — fetch ~50 candidates, rerank, keep 5.

Stuffing in too many chunks. Pasting 20 or 30 passages feels safe but buries the real answer in noise, confuses the model, and quietly burns tokens. A handful of clean, on-topic chunks almost always beats a big sloppy pile.

Relying on meaning search alone for exact terms. Semantic search blurs precise strings like part numbers, error codes, and rare names. Add keyword search and blend the two (hybrid search), or those literal lookups will silently fail.

Not rewriting conversational follow-ups. "What about the cheaper one?" embeds into nonsense on its own. Without a query-rewrite step that folds in the chat history, your search looks for the wrong thing and returns garbage.

Embedding queries with a different model than the documents. The question and the stored chunks must be turned into numbers by the same embedding model, or you are comparing two incompatible number systems and matches become random.

Never measuring retrieval on its own. When answers are bad, people blame the model. Test the pipeline separately: for known questions, does the correct chunk actually appear in the top results? Fix retrieval before touching the prompt or the model.
exercise:
Build a tiny pipeline and prove reranking matters. Take 30–50 short text chunks (a few blog posts or doc pages sliced up). Embed them, store them, and write a retrieve(query) that returns the top 10 by embedding similarity. Now write five real questions whose answers you know live in specific chunks. For each, check: did the correct chunk land in the top 3? Record your hit rate.

Stretch goal: Add a reranking pass — fetch the top 20 by embedding, then re-score those 20 with a small cross-encoder reranker, and keep the new top 3. Re-run your five questions and compare hit rates before and after. Write one sentence explaining why a model that reads the question and chunk together ranks them better than one that scored each chunk in isolation.
solution: Hint: Embedding search is a bi-encoder — it turns the question into numbers and each chunk into numbers separately, then measures distance. It never compares them side by side, so it misses subtle relevance. A reranker (cross-encoder) feeds the question and one chunk into the model at the same time, letting it weigh how the exact words interact, which is why it ranks far more accurately. You will usually see your top-3 hit rate climb after reranking, especially on questions where the right chunk used phrasing different from the query. Use a lightweight reranker like a bge-reranker or a hosted rerank API — and only rerank the ~20 you already fetched, since reranking everything would be slow and pointless.
summary: A retrieval pipeline is the plumbing that fetches the right text for a RAG system: process the query, embed it, search (ideally hybrid — meaning plus keywords blended with RRF), rerank the rough candidates with a sharper model, then assemble a clean, compact context for the LLM. The model only sees what the pipeline brings, so quality lives or dies here. Keep a handful of focused chunks over a big sloppy pile, and always measure whether the correct chunk lands in the top results.

keyTerms.def: The multi-stage process that takes a user's question and returns the most relevant stored text for a model to answer with — query processing, embedding, search, reranking, and context assembly.
Rephrasing a question (often folding in chat history) into a self-contained form before searching, so vague follow-ups like 'what about the second one?' become searchable.
Combining meaning-based semantic search with literal keyword search, then blending the two ranked lists, so you catch both paraphrased matches and exact strings like codes or names.
A simple recipe for merging two ranked result lists into one, rewarding items that rank high in either list — no fiddly weight-tuning required.
A slower, sharper model that reads the question and a candidate chunk together and scores their relevance as a pair, used to refine a rough first-pass search down to the best few results.
The fast embedding approach that turns the query and each chunk into numbers separately and compares distances — quick but less accurate than a cross-encoder that reads them together.
How many chunks the pipeline keeps and passes to the model. Too few starves it of facts; too many adds noise and cost. Often 5–10 clean chunks.
The final step of stitching the chosen chunks into one clean block, removing duplicates, and fitting it inside the model's context window before sending it to the model.
The larger pool of rough matches (often ~50) pulled by the fast first search, which the reranker then narrows down to the final handful.
